author: @BluesDoctor
text:
Well done mate. To me it follows with the comparison between pragmatic and hermeneutics. Pragmatic methodologies support reproducibility, reproducibility will occasionally fail or become outdated for something better. Failure with a proven methodology is the result of an incomplete methodology, some contamination or defect not inclusive to the methodology. Philosophy does not change methodologies do. A chair that fails after repeated 300 pound loads exposes a flaw in methodology, or at least the limits of methodology. 

Sometimes happy accidents occur, usually not, but the unexpected is discovery. In both cases pragmatic methodology must yield to hermeneutics, rethinking and reformulating methodologies. 

Investigative experiments are usually a collection of methodologies with a focus on discovery in formulating new methodology. 

Uncovering new truths where truth is efficacy of facts. Efficacy is belief subject to change with new information or clarification. Sometimes hard to remember when methodologies have become dogma.

To me you are pointing to hermeneutics and the keys to reformulating beliefs leading to new methodologies. 

The delight I receive from watching your work and progress is that rare example of hermeneutics in science well executed.

Cheers mate, you do exceptional work and unselfish sharing of strategies and discovery.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: BluesDoctor Thanks very much for that mate - i enjoyed reading it and it make me think - basically i think you are right and spot on - cheers

author: @E.lectricityNorth
text:
Reminds me of a response that I've seen from you a few times, regarding theories...I believe it went something like this, "Everything's a theory, mate". 

Good explanation!  At some point, we have to rest on the results that time, experimentation and practice have provided.  When the whole game changes and our previously useful inductive reasoning no longer functions, I believe that is known as a "game changer"!

replies.text: E. Lectricity theory is just a lack of practice mate lol - I think you are absolutely right here
